policy: Raw references are local storyboard cache only and are ignored by git.
Use references only for camera angle, blocking, silhouette rhythm, depth, negative space, and gag timing.
Do not copy original characters, logos, readable titles, actor likenesses, captions, or exact costumes.
No CP/romance implication: no hearts, red strings, love arrows, blush, confession, date, couple-poster framing, hand-holding, hugging, or face-close intimacy.

references.title: Community pizza fire reaction composition
references.licenseNote: Reference-only meme image; not committed and not copied into game art.
references.compositionUse: Doorway entry into a scene that has already gone off the rails; foreground newcomer, chaotic background, instant reaction comedy.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the original actors, room, fire, pizza box, show logo, or live-action look.
references.promptCue: use the doorway-arrival chaos skeleton: one character enters/catches the other in a too-obvious stakeout, with the core prop already causing a tiny incident.

references.title: Is This a Pigeon? pointing mislabel composition
references.licenseNote: Reference-only meme image; not committed and not copied into game art.
references.compositionUse: One figure points at the wrong thing with absolute confidence while the other silently judges the mistaken label.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the original anime character, butterfly, caption layout, labels, or readable text.
references.promptCue: use the confident misidentification skeleton: one character points at a glowing record/name echo, the other checks the core prop with deadpan disbelief.

references.title: Daily Struggle / Two Buttons decision composition
references.licenseNote: Reference-only meme image; not committed and not copied into game art.
references.compositionUse: A character caught between two equally wrong options, framed as a route-choice decision gag.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the original man, table, button labels, red buttons, or readable text.
references.promptCue: use the two-choice route skeleton: two unreadable quest paths or markers collide, and both characters realize the navigation logic is nonsense.

references.title: A Trip to the Moon rocket impact frame
references.licenseNote: Public-domain early cinema reference via Wikimedia Commons.
references.compositionUse: Absurd central impact: one object has crashed into the focal point, making the whole frame instantly legible as a failed experiment.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the moon face, rocket design, historical engraving texture, title, or exact layout.
references.promptCue: use the absurd impact skeleton: the self-made bug/core prop lands dead center like an impossible experiment while one character celebrates and the other audits the damage.

references.title: Safety Last! clock-hanging frame
references.licenseNote: Public-domain silent film reference via Wikimedia Commons.
references.compositionUse: Urgent lateness and vertical peril around a giant clock; great for missed timing and arriving after the key beat.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy Harold Lloyd, the city facade, exact clock face, or live-action monochrome look.
references.promptCue: use the giant deadline-object skeleton: one character clings to or rushes around a huge scene prop while the other points out they are already third to arrive.

references.title: Sherlock Jr. poster projection/detective composition
references.licenseNote: Public-domain silent film poster reference via Wikimedia Commons.
references.compositionUse: Detective/film-within-film framing: a big framed view, a smaller observer figure, and a clue that invites shared inspection.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy Buster Keaton, poster typography, magnifying-glass branding, or exact costume.
references.promptCue: use the projection-screen observation skeleton: two characters study light/reflection/core prop like a tiny cinema clue, offset rather than posed as a couple.

references.title: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari warped poster composition
references.licenseNote: Public-domain silent film poster reference via Wikimedia Commons.
references.compositionUse: Expressionist empty-space framing: tilted architecture, long shadows, and one small lit island inside an odd night scene.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the poster lettering, character silhouettes, exact buildings, or horror mood too literally.
references.promptCue: use the warped quiet-stage skeleton: large empty negative space, one lamp/core prop as a third comic presence, characters slightly apart in a deadpan pause.

references.title: Nosferatu shadow silhouette composition
references.licenseNote: Public-domain-inspired silhouette reference via Wikimedia Commons SVG render.
references.compositionUse: Huge impossible shadow on the wall/stairs, perfect for 'is that you or not you?' overlap jokes.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the vampire silhouette, claws, stairs, or exact horror iconography.
references.promptCue: use the oversized-shadow skeleton: one character points at a suspicious overlapping shadow while the other records it as a new title-worthy incident.

references.title: The General train chase poster composition
references.licenseNote: Public-domain silent film poster reference via Wikimedia Commons.
references.compositionUse: Diagonal chase/action poster: vehicle or prop rushes through frame while characters react in comic panic.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the train design, actor likeness, poster text, or exact chase layout.
references.promptCue: use the diagonal action skeleton: a short dialogue combo turns into a fast prop chase or split-screen impact line with clear comic timing.

references.title: Steamboat Willie wheel frame
references.licenseNote: Public-domain early animation reference via Wikimedia Commons.
references.compositionUse: Simple readable circular prop with elastic cartoon acting; useful as backup for slapstick object control.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy Mickey Mouse, the wheel design, monochrome style, or any Disney branding.
references.promptCue: use the circular-control skeleton: a character confidently operates the wrong object while the other reacts as the bit escalates.

references.title: Metropolis Maschinenmensch machine-stage composition
references.licenseNote: Historic film still reference via Wikimedia Commons; reference-only composition use.
references.compositionUse: Central machine altar and symmetrical light columns, useful for server-room/debug drama without romance framing.
references.avoidCopy: Do not copy the Maschinenmensch design, set, costume, lighting arrangement exactly, or film identity.
references.promptCue: use the central-machine skeleton: core prop becomes a dramatic bug/debug altar while the characters act as tester and incident reporter.
